summary: Cantrill reflects on re-reading Tracy Kidder's 'The Soul of a New Machine' decades after it first inspired him as an undergraduate. He discovers that passages about organizational politics, leadership, and team dynamics that meant little to him as a student now resonate deeply with his career experiences. The book's exploration of why engineers build things—and why they persist through difficulty—leads him to conclude that shared mission and mutual trust are what enable teams to accomplish extraordinary feats.
key_points: The Soul of a New Machine should be considered computing's literary foundation—our Odyssey
Re-reading reveals new meaning: organizational rivalries and leadership complexities now resonate with career experience
Trust is risk, and risk-averse organizations struggle to foster mutual trust despite its obvious advantages
Engineers persist through difficulty not for ego or money, but because they are bonded by shared mission
The soul of what we build is shared—we do not endeavor alone but with like-minded individuals
money_quotes: I – like so many before and after me – found the book to be life changing: by telling the stories of the people behind the machine, the book showed the creative passion among engineers that might otherwise appear anodyne.

In any engineering career of sufficient length, a beloved project will at some point be shelved or killed – and the moment will be sufficiently traumatic to be seared into collective memory and memorialized in local lore.
West is right that trust is risk – and risk-averse organizations can really struggle to foster mutual trust, despite its obvious advantages.

When the problem is challenging and success uncertain, the endurance of engineers cannot be explained by these factors alone; regardless of why they start, engineers persist because they are bonded by a shared mission.
On teams that have a shared mission, mutual trust and ample grit, incredible things become possible.
The Soul of a New Machine serves to remind us that the soul of what we build is, above all, shared – that we do not endeavor alone but rather with a group of like-minded individuals.
key_insight: The endurance of engineering teams through difficult challenges stems not from individual motivation but from the bonds of shared mission and mutual trust.
